title: Cat Head Tilt to the Right: Causes and Urgent Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your cat tilting their head to the right? Learn about vestibular syndrome, why it is a high-priority emergency, and how to help your cat now.
content_html:
TL;DR: A sudden head tilt in cats is a high-priority emergency that indicates a problem with the vestibular system and requires immediate veterinary evaluation. Capturing a short video of your cat's movements can provide critical information to help the triage team determine the cause and severity.

What does it mean if my cat has a head tilt?

A head tilt, where your cat holds their head rotated to one side—specifically the right side in this case—is most often a sign of an issue with the vestibular system. This system is responsible for maintaining balance and orientation. When it is disrupted, either in the inner ear or the brain, the cat loses their sense of equilibrium, leading to the characteristic tilt.

Is a head tilt in cats a medical emergency?

  • Urgency Level: High. A sudden head tilt should always be treated as a medical emergency.
  • While some causes, like a middle ear infection, are easily treatable, others can be more serious, such as feline vestibular disease, inflammatory polyps, or neurological issues like tumors or strokes.
  • Immediate veterinary evaluation is required if your cat is also exhibiting unsteadiness, falling, or rapid eye movements (nystagmus) to prevent further distress or permanent damage.

How can taking a photo or video help with my cat's triage?

  • Taking a quick photo or a 10-second video of your cat’s head position and eye movements is incredibly helpful for a triage nurse or veterinarian.
  • Visual evidence allows the medical team to see the exact angle of the tilt and check for involuntary eye twitching.
  • This helps distinguish between peripheral (inner ear) and central (brain) vestibular issues.
  • Providing this information ensures your cat receives the correct priority level upon arrival at the clinic.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In cats, head tilt can be a sign of vestibular disease, often manifesting with dysequilibrium, a tendency to circle, fall, or roll to one side, and abnormal eye position. Unilateral lesions of the vestibulocochlear nerves typically result in a head tilt toward the side of the lesion, accompanied by spontaneous positional, horizontal, or rotary nystagmus. Middle to older cats may develop ear canal tumors leading to neurologic signs such as head tilt, deafness, ataxia, nystagmus, facial nerve palsy, Horner syndrome, and third eyelid protrusion. Urgent diagnostics such as a brain-stem auditory evoked response (BAER) test may be necessary.

Chapter: Oncology, Otolaryngology, Neurology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 534)

faqs.question: Can a cat's head tilt go away on its own?
faqs.answer: While some cases of 'idiopathic vestibular disease' improve over time, you should never wait to see. Many causes of head tilt are painful or progressive and require medication or professional cleaning to resolve.

faqs.question: What are other symptoms to look for with a right-side head tilt?
faqs.answer: Look for flickering eye movements, walking in circles toward the right, nausea (drooling), or a sudden loss of appetite. These all point toward a balance system disturbance.

faqs.question: Is a head tilt painful for my cat?
faqs.answer: The tilt itself may not be painful, but the underlying cause—such as a deep ear infection—can be extremely painful. Furthermore, the resulting dizziness and vertigo are very distressing for cats.
